sentences.text: My name is Uri Rantz, R-A-N-T-Z.
U-R-I.
The same.
Yes.
A lot of things.
But Peterman-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
What did your parents call you?
Utzi.
What is your date of birth?
And old does that make you today?
I was born in Lutsk, which was, when I was born, was Poland.
And now it belongs to the Ukrainian Republic.
My father's name was Samuel.
My mother's name was Genia.
I think is from Getel-- became Genia.
Juniczman.
My mother-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] except that she was a beautiful woman-- she was-- she comes from a very Zionist family.
Let's see, her brother was the leader of the Hatzalah, which is a revisionist, grown-up movement.
And the work-- he was-- he was in the Knesset in Israel.
He was sent by the British to the concentration camp in-- in Africa.
And then she had another sister in Rowne.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And my cousin, whom I didn't see 50 years.
We met about 10 years ago-- we met in Russia.
And she has another-- it's actually a half-sister in Israel. that she's-- because my grandfather remarried.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And we had a grandmother, the mother's mother, living with us in-- in Lutsk.
Brucha.
My father came from a very large family.
I am named after my grandfather-- my father's father-- who died before I was born.
He had six brothers, and this is from the same-- same father and same mother.
Then she had-- he had another two, well, a sister and a brother, from the father, and from a different mother.
As a young man, he was a revolutionist.
He was sent by the [NON-ENGLISH] in exile.
His picture was printed in 1905, and forward here in the United States, about his acting.
Then when he-- after he married my mother-- of course, he became a settled man.
And he had all kinds of different businesses.
Are you interested in them?
In the beginning, he was a-- he had a store of [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] tobacco-- no kind of accessories to tobacco, smoking.
In 1931, he became a director of a bank, which was a Jewish bank.
In the Wall Street Crash, the bank-- the bank when bankrupted.
Then he was back in a different business also, in selling all kinds of-- I don't know how it would be English word-- women's accessories, and so forth.
And when I left home in 19-- one thing, yes, which the reason that I went to Russia is that he wasn't [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a war prisoner in the Germans, during the First World War, by the Germans.
And when the war broke out with Germany, we decided that we are not going to stay home.
We are going to out to Russia.
And it is where I am when the war broke out in 1939.
I was ready to go, but then there was the Settlement Pact between Hitler and Stalin.
They divided Poland, and now part of Poland became part of the Soviet Union.
Well, Lutsk was a city of about 25,000, 28,000 Jews.
There were maybe 5,000, 7,000 Gentiles.
They had all kind-- all Jewishes which existed at that time in the Jewish world, they existed in our town.
All kinds of parties, political parties.
Of course, the only religion that we knew was the Orthodox religion.
But here, we can more orthodox or less orthodox, but we didn't have any of that.
And we had all-- we had Hebrew schools.
We had schools by the leftist, Bund-- by the leftist Bund in Yiddish.
In town, we had high schools-- gymnasiums.
We had-- which I attended, it was in Polish, but I attended by Jews only.
Then it was a Russian gymnasium, then it was a Ukrainian gymnasium, and there was two Polish gymnasiums.
I'm go to--I went to the public school.
I went been to Tarbut, which was everything in Hebrew, and Polish was a foreign language.
After I finished Tarbut, which was the public school, at age  of 12, I went to gymnasium.
And I finished regency, which we called matura.
On top of everything, when I was in Tarbut, I had everyday coming from a Rabbi teaching me Jewish religious things.
And I went right quite deep after [NON-ENGLISH] started, the mission [NON-ENGLISH].
Every day for all the years after school, I had the rabbi come over.
There were-- I had two brothers, younger brothers.
They both were killed together with my parents.
And we had a big--
The second brother was Herschel, and the youngest was Raphael.
And they were this-- there's a complicated thing, over Herschel went out, but then my cousin-- he was with my cousin-- he became wounded on the way to Russia.
So he took the cousin back home and that's where he stayed.
He remained during the German occupation.
Yes.
As a matter of fact, my grandfather built the synagogue.
It was a [NON-ENGLISH].
It's [NON-ENGLISH] Olek was a-- like you have your [NON-ENGLISH], and the others was [NON-ENGLISH].
And he was a member there, and then my father was a member, and I was going with my father all the holidays and Saturdays.
My father wasn't going every day to shul.
Sure.
Well, it was not like in America, of course.
It was-- they called you up to the Torah, you read the Torah, and then it was a Kiddush.
And at home, was some of a few family members, some family member with another little reception.
But nothing big, nothing elaborate.
Well, typical, it was-- depends on my age, of course.
In the beginning, I had to comply with certain rules.
I was told to do, I had to do it.
When I was older, a little, we compromised.
Of course, we always had the Shabbos meal, the Shabbos-- everybody was at the Shabbos dinner.
But after dinner, it depends on what age.
In the beginning, we were going to visit relatives, or they came to visit us.
And after a while, when I was older, I already was going to my own friends for-- to visit or to whatever.
No.
Of course we knew-- I was always very interested in politics.
We were reading, we were listening, and we had a free press, and-- and we knew exactly what was going on in Germany.
Yes.
We had-- I-- we had a three-storey building that we lived.
Each of my uncles had a quarter, like we had the main floor, my uncle had the top floor, and the other corner, the other uncle had the main floor, and the aunt with the children.
We were 12 all together, each one had three children.
And we had disagreements, in a sense, that some of the [NON-ENGLISH], which is the Gentiles, they attacked us, but since we were a group of 12.
We waited for them.
They never came twice to our street anymore.
On top of it, in Poland, was an organization-- a Fascist organization-- which I'll discuss-- the initials ND, Index.
At one point, they were supposed to come-- they had pogroms in certain cities.
Then all the Jewish organizations, the Jewish [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] longshoremen, the [NON-ENGLISH], everyone was organized, and we waited for them and they didn't go far in the city.
They went back the way they came.
But it was in the last year before the war, was already all kinds of actions by the Polish government against the Jewish, like in colleges, they had to sit in a certain-- certain site.
It was a numurus nullus, and numurus clausus-- numurus was that they weren't accepted at all, in certain-- in certain colleges, or they were accepted, they were very limited.
Since I wanted to go for engineering, but I couldn't go to engineering school in Poland.
This was my plan.
But it-- the same goes for medicine.
It's why many people-- of the Jews who could afford went to Italy, went to France, went to Belgium.
As a matter, my first wife, which I met in Rome, she went from Bratislava to Rome, and she finished medicine in Italy.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] this is the-- yes, and the-- and this is also was the reason that we had to-- it was a very strong movement, a Zionist movement.
We could agree with all kinds of politics of each movement, but the principle of things, we knew that Poland-- the Jews had no future in Poland.

Because of-- they started, also, to boycott the Jewish stores, and all environment became a very much-- of course very much were by the German influence, but people don't need any-- too big complicated reasons to become anti-Semitic or something like that.
Under Russian occupation, we were supposed to be shipped out of our town, because people-- let's say-- who have-- who-- so-called capitalists, but, of course, it was exaggeration.
So we were supposed to be shipped out of our town.
But as my father had a background of a revolutionary, we wrote to Stalin.
We got an answer from Stalin that if they had the background-- so they left us, and they gave us back our house.
And he started to work for the city government.
He was a supplier of wood and everything for the city.
I went to a college, they didn't-- which was a physics-mathematics.
And until the war broke out, I was in this college.
Life became normal, and not normal, because they didn't change to a communist system overnight.
It was-- it took-- they were-- they had all kinds of plans.
Of course, the war disrupted the plans, but a lot of Jews felt much better under the Soviet domination than under the Polish discrimination.
When war broke out, my father wasn't home, so I had to stay with the mother with the kids.
We had to go from our house to some relatives, cousins, because all the-- we lived close to the main street, and all the traffic of tanks was going 24 hours to the front.
And we just-- my mother didn't have the nerves to support this, so we went to-- to a cousin's, that they lived on a side street.
And we stayed with them.
Until my father came back-- when my father came back, on the third day of the war-- the war broke out the 22nd of June, 1941.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Anyhow, the 25th, I left home.
He was back.
I took my few things, and they gave me some money, and I left with a friend of mine towards Russia.
I had a bicycle.
He had a bicycle.
We were traveling on bicycles in this.
We are making maybe a thousand miles, I don't know.
Until I came to a city by the name [NON-ENGLISH].
My dad said it was organized train for the refugees, which took me all the way to Stalingrad.
You want me to continue?
But the train, itself, this wasn't everything just took a train.
It was all kind of things were happening.
I mean, on the way out of the city, we were attacked by the German's planes, and all kind of things, which as long as I can talk about it means that nothing happened to me.
And when we came to Stalingrad, they took us to about 100 miles from Stalingrad to kolkhoz to work.
We were 10.
Somehow we find ourselves 10 from the same-- about four or five from Lutsk, and the others were from little cities near Lutsk.
All Jewish.
And we started to work among the Cossacks.
We lived on the Don.
We lived among those Cossacks famous for all the pogroms, and there is a book by Sholokhov, and this was exactly, we lived there with those people.
In the beginning, we were Pollocks, Pollocks, Pollocks.
And after a couple of weeks, when we became friendly with us-- in Russia at that time, they didn't ask religion.
They asked what religion we are, we told them we are Jewish.
They couldn't believe.
They said, they were surprised.
They said, you are like we.

They never saw a Jew, but for them, a Jew was some kind of-- I became-- again, which was a little bit luck, or whatever it is-- the bookkeeper of the-- not the bookkeeper, but the function of a bookkeeper, and the function of a-- I have to explain the system, how the whole system worked in Russia.
People weren't paid for work.
They were-- they were paid in form of working days.
They had an algorithm-- if you cut, let's say, a 10 square meter of field, you get one working day.
You cut-- you cut 20 or 30, you have the multiple of that.
I was the one who had to check what other people were producing.
It was part of my job.
And-- and we were settled, everything was going well.
Until the Germans started to come closer to Stalingrad.
And we knew, we don't-- we lived on the Don, Don River.
There was no other communication except by river, because the next station was like 20 miles away, and there was no transportation, private, and everything.
So the only way to go out of there was by the Don.
And the Don freezes in winter, so we had to go out before the winter.
Of course, thousands of people were waiting in line and-- and-- and we didn't have a chance to do it.
But God works in mysterious ways.
It came-- a barge with coal-- so the manager of the station told us, if you unload the barge--the coal barge, you'll have a trip to Stalingrad with the barge.
Of course, I don't have to tell you we took the job.
We did it.
Whatever-- it took us a day and a half.
We unloaded the coal.
And then we went to Stalingrad with that barge.
But before we went to Stalingrad, before we arrived to Stalingrad, the barge stopped in a little town-- a little village.
So we walked into the store of the village, and the manager of the store happened to be Jewish.
We told him what-- what we are doing, where we want to go.
He said, don't go to Stalingrad.
Stop before Stalingrad, because everything is going-- all the transportation is organized before.
So we stopped before Stalingrad.

And we did-- the transporter was still empty, and we were the first in that-- we had half of it was a cattle wagons-- half and then three families from Odessa, Jewish families just happened to be-- came in in Stalingrad.
And we, for three months, took us to go from Stalingrad to Asia, which usually is a trip of a day or something.
But it was war, and all kinds of things.
And every transport had priority on our transport.
And this way we arrived to Tashkent.
This was in 1940-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] '41, but in November or December.
Then from there again, they took us to a kolkhoz to work-- about south of Bukhara, and so on and so forth.
I mentioned Bukhara because it was the famous Jewish musical, and a well-known Jewish community, which had lived in Bukhara.
And many of them, or most of them, had been to Palestine at that time before the war.
And we stayed in that kolkhoz, and the name of the kolkhoz was [NON-ENGLISH], which doesn't mean anything.
And until the Germans were beaten in Stalingrad, and they started to retreat, then we decided to come-- to go closer home-- from-- to travel to-- back to-- to Russia.
Tashkent is is in Uzbekistan.
We tried to, in the meantime, also, to cross the border to Iran, from there to Israel, because some people succeeded.
But we were caught.
We were lucky that we could escape.
And so this was in '42, and '43, so we started to move to-- closer to Russia, and we came to [NON-ENGLISH], I got sick in [NON-ENGLISH].
It was-- and I ended up in a working battalion.
They had military army, and they had working army-- since I was a Polish citizen, still, originally.
So they sent us to working battalions.
And I worked in a factory, which made their own mines.
And I ended up to work in the-- in the poice, in the military police of the factory.
Since while I was in college, I had a good experience in the basis of communism, because in every military group, there was a political agitator.
But our political agitator was a drunkard.
So he gave me-- so he found out that I know-- I'm familiar with the-- with those things, that I used to substitute.
And once I made a joke about Stalin, and I was arrested next day.
When they took me from my--from the-- our factory was out of town.
Took me-- they had to take me down-- well, they had the secret police to take me to their quarters-- headquarters.
So I went to the toilet, and I jumped from the window from the toilet.
I jumped out.
I still have signs here.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I schlepped myself to a village, and people were nice people.
I knew people in that village, and I was a few days recuperating.
And here comes in another little thing I would think is fate.

While I was working in the-- on the-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] military police of the factory, I had a girlfriend, and her father had a Russian girlfriend-- that happened to be the secretary of the Communist Party-- one of the secretaries.
And he was in charge of all the forest, all the forest and all the mines in the state.
So I came to say goodbye to her, that I'm going away, because I'm in trouble.
She said, let's talk to father.
So we went to talk to the father.
And the father said, you know, they just freed all the Polish citizens from the camps.
Because when the Russians came to us in '41, we had a lot of refugees from Poland, from the other side of the Polish--of the Russian border.
And that one night, they put them all into trains and took them to Siberia to-- to come up to the northern-- to the Arctic.
And the all worked in forests cutting wood in the forest, and worked in mines.
Shall I stop?
